name: Qatl/Qisas/Diyat Core
formula: Qatl Type → Proof Route → Wali Status → Waiver/Compounding → Diyat Valuation/Payment

els.name: Qatl Shibh-i-Amd and Debarment from Succession
els.sub: S.315/316/317 PPC — the intermediate homicide category and its inheritance consequence
els.desc: Qatl Shibh-i-Amd (S.315) is committed where a person, intending to cause bodily/mental harm (not death), causes death by means or an act not ordinarily likely to cause death — the Code's own Illustration is striking a person with a stick or stone not ordinarily fatal, where death nonetheless results. S.316 punishes this with Diyat liability and up to 25 years' Ta'zir imprisonment. S.317 then applies a succession-law consequence common to both Qatl-i-Amd and Qatl Shibh-i-Amd: a person who commits either is debarred from succeeding to the victim's estate as an heir or beneficiary under a will.
els.legal: Qatl Shibh-i-Amd occupies the conceptual space between Qatl-i-Amd (S.300's intentional/knowing killing) and Qatl-i-Khata (S.318's mistake-based killing, covered next): the offender genuinely intended only to cause harm, using means objectively unlikely to cause death, yet death resulted anyway. This maps onto a mental state lower than any of the four S.300 Niyyat clauses but higher than pure accident — a useful comparative point against the Murder module's existing Niyyat framework, since Shibh-i-Amd sits in the gap S.300's clauses do not reach. S.317's debarment-from-succession rule applies a clean, almost automatic civil consequence on top of the criminal punishment, reflecting the general legal principle that a person should not profit from their own wrongdoing against the very person whose estate they would inherit.
els.cite: S.315, S.316, S.317 PPC and S.315's Illustration — official text. Cross-reference Murder S.300 Niyyat element — Qatl Shibh-i-Amd occupies the mental-state space below S.300's four clauses.
els.ll: S.315, S.316, S.317 PPC — official text

landmark.holding: This module corrects a factual error identified and fixed this session: the Murder module’s existing S.311 element previously stated the honour-killing override as ‘minimum 14 years, up to 25 years’ Taʼzir. Verified directly against the Bare Act, S.311’s actual text provides a general Taʼzir ceiling of death/life/up to 14 years, with the honour-killing proviso specifically mandating life imprisonment — not a 14–25 year range. This has been corrected in the Murder module’s S.311 element as part of this build. No fabricated landmark case citation is offered for this module specifically; the procedural Qisas/Diyat mechanics here are most commonly litigated as part of the same proceedings already captured by the landmark cases logged under the Murder module (Qandeel Baloch, ZA Bhutto, Mumtaz Qadri, Safia Bano).

sq.q: A, intending only to cause hurt, strikes Z with a stick — an object which in the ordinary course of nature is not likely to cause death. Z unexpectedly dies as a result. Apply S.315 and its own Illustration. What is A guilty of, and how does this differ from Qatl-i-Amd under S.300?
sq.reveal: This is the Code's own Illustration to S.315 directly: A is guilty of Qatl Shibh-i-Amd, not Qatl-i-Amd. The critical distinguishing feature from S.300 is the nature of the means used: a stick is, in the ordinary course of nature, not likely to cause death — this is precisely why none of S.300's four Niyyat clauses (which all require either intent to kill, intent to cause likely-fatal injury, intent to cause injury sufficient in the ordinary course to cause death, or knowledge of an imminently dangerous act) are satisfied. A's mental state (mere intent to cause harm) combined with objectively non-lethal means produces the intermediate Shibh-i-Amd category instead.
sq.cite: S.315 PPC Illustration — stick/stone as means not ordinarily likely to cause death, distinguishing Shibh-i-Amd from S.300 Qatl-i-Amd.

sq.q: An offender is convicted of Qatl Shibh-i-Amd against their own parent, and would otherwise be entitled to inherit from that parent's estate as the closest surviving heir. Apply S.317. Does the conviction affect the inheritance?
sq.reveal: Yes. S.317 expressly debars a person who commits Qatl-i-Amd OR Qatl Shibh-i-Amd from succeeding to the victim's estate as an heir or beneficiary under a will — the debarment applies regardless of which of these two categories the conviction falls under, and regardless of how close the family relationship otherwise was. The offender forfeits any inheritance rights they would have held against the very person they killed, even where the killing falls short of full Qatl-i-Amd.
sq.cite: S.317 PPC — debarment from succession applies to both Qatl-i-Amd and Qatl Shibh-i-Amd convictions.

sq.q: Compare the punishment exposure under S.316 (Qatl Shibh-i-Amd: Diyat plus up to 25 years Ta'zir) to the Murder module's existing S.302(c) tier (Ta'zir imprisonment up to 25 years where Qisas is not applicable in law). Is Shibh-i-Amd simply a lesser-included version of S.302(c), or are they structurally distinct punishment tracks?
sq.reveal: They are structurally distinct, even though the imprisonment ceiling happens to coincide at 25 years. S.302(c) operates within the Qatl-i-Amd framework specifically — it is one of three punishment tracks (alongside Qisas and Ta'zir-death) available once Qatl-i-Amd itself, with its S.300 Niyyat requirement, has been established. S.316, by contrast, governs an entirely separate homicide category (Shibh-i-Amd) that exists precisely because the S.300 Niyyat requirement is NOT met. Critically, S.316 always includes mandatory Diyat liability alongside the optional Ta'zir imprisonment, whereas S.302(c) is a pure imprisonment track with no separate Diyat component built into that specific clause. A student conflating the two as 'the same 25-year tier' would miss that one requires proof of full Qatl-i-Amd intent and the other specifically applies where that intent is absent.
sq.cite: S.316 PPC vs Murder module S.302(c) — structurally distinct tracks despite a coincidentally identical imprisonment ceiling; S.316 always carries mandatory Diyat, S.302(c) does not.

els.name: Qatl-i-Khata — Killing by Mistake
els.sub: S.318/319/320 PPC — mistake of act or fact, including the negligent-driving variant
els.desc: Qatl-i-Khata (S.318) is committed where a person, without any intention to cause death or harm, causes death either by mistake of act or mistake of fact — the Code's own Illustrations cover both: aiming at a deer and accidentally killing a bystander instead (mistake of act), and shooting at what is believed to be a boar but turns out to be a human (mistake of fact). S.319 punishes the base form with Diyat liability, with an additional proviso allowing up to 5 years' Ta'zir where the Khata arose from rash or negligent conduct other than driving. S.320 separately and more severely addresses Qatl-i-Khata specifically by rash or negligent driving, mandating Diyat plus up to 10 years' imprisonment, having regard to the facts and circumstances.
els.legal: Qatl-i-Khata sits at the lowest end of the homicide-culpability spectrum built into Chapter XVI — genuinely no intention to cause death or harm at all, distinguishing it sharply from both Qatl-i-Amd (S.300's intentional/knowing framework) and Qatl Shibh-i-Amd (S.315's intent-to-harm-but-not-kill framework). The two Illustrations are carefully chosen to show the two distinct species of mistake the section covers: mistake of act (the bodily movement itself goes wrong — aiming at one target, hitting another) and mistake of fact (the bodily movement goes exactly as intended, but a false belief about the nature of the target was held). S.320's specific, harsher treatment of negligent-driving deaths reflects a deliberate legislative carve-out responding to the particular social prevalence and seriousness of road-traffic fatalities, treating them as warranting double the maximum Ta'zir exposure compared to other rash/negligent Khata under S.319's proviso.
els.cite: S.318, S.319, S.320 PPC and S.318's two Illustrations — official text. Cross-reference General Explanations S.39 (Voluntarily) — Qatl-i-Khata is precisely the category of death-causing conduct that falls outside S.39's voluntariness definition, since neither intention nor knowledge of likely death is present.
els.ll: S.318, S.319, S.320 PPC — official text

sq.q: A aims at what A genuinely and reasonably believes to be a wild boar in thick brush and fires, intending only to hunt the animal. The target turns out to be a human being, who dies. Apply S.318 and its own Illustration (b). What category of Qatl is this, and why is it not Qatl-i-Amd?
sq.reveal: This is the Code's own Illustration (b) to S.318 directly: A is guilty of Qatl-i-Khata, specifically the mistake-of-fact variant. It is not Qatl-i-Amd because none of the S.300 Niyyat clauses are satisfied — A had no intention to cause death or harm to any person at all, and A's belief, however mistaken, was that the target was an animal, not a human being. The entire mental architecture of S.300 (which all four clauses require some form of intent or knowledge directed at causing death or serious harm to a person) is simply absent on these facts.
sq.cite: S.318 PPC Illustration (b) — mistake of fact (wrong belief about the nature of the target) as one of the two Qatl-i-Khata variants.

sq.q: A driver, while genuinely driving with rash disregard for pedestrian safety (speeding through a clearly marked pedestrian crossing), strikes and kills a pedestrian. Apply S.320 specifically, rather than S.319's general rash/negligent proviso. Why does this fact pattern fall under S.320 rather than S.319?
sq.reveal: S.319's proviso for rash or negligent conduct explicitly carves out driving — it applies only to rash/negligent Khata 'other than rash or negligent driving.' Since this fact pattern specifically involves driving, S.320 governs instead, mandating Diyat plus up to 10 years' imprisonment (double S.319's 5-year ceiling for other forms of rash/negligent Khata), reflecting the legislature's specific, heightened concern with road-traffic fatalities as their own distinct sub-category requiring separate, harsher treatment.
sq.cite: S.319 PPC proviso's express exclusion of driving, and S.320 PPC as the specific, harsher provision governing rash/negligent-driving Khata.

sq.q: Does Qatl-i-Khata under S.318 require that the death-causing act itself be lawful, or can Khata arise even from an unlawful act, provided there was no intention to cause death or harm?
sq.reveal: S.318's text requires only the absence of intention to cause death or harm, and that death results from mistake of act or mistake of fact — it does not on its face require the underlying act to have been lawful. This distinguishes Qatl-i-Khata from Qatl Bis-Sabab (S.321, covered separately), which specifically requires an unlawful act as its trigger. A genuinely lawful but mistaken act (such as the hunting scenarios in S.318's own Illustrations) falls naturally within S.318; whether an unlawful but non-death-intending act resulting in death falls under S.318's mistake framework or S.321's unlawful-act framework would depend on the precise causal mechanism on the facts — S.321 specifically requires the unlawful act itself to 'become a cause' of death, a structurally distinct causal pathway from S.318's mistake-based pathway.
sq.cite: S.318 PPC (mistake-based, no lawfulness requirement stated) vs S.321 PPC Qatl Bis-Sabab (unlawful-act-based) — structurally distinct causal pathways to an unintended death.

els.name: Qatl Bis-Sabab — Death Caused by an Unlawful Act
els.sub: S.321/322 PPC — death as an indirect consequence of unlawful conduct
els.desc: Qatl Bis-Sabab (S.321) is committed where a person, without any intention to cause death or harm to anyone, does an unlawful act that becomes a cause of another person's death. The Code's own Illustration is unlawfully digging a pit in a public thoroughfare without intending harm to anyone, where a passer-by falls in and dies. S.322 punishes this with Diyat liability alone — no additional Ta'zir component is provided for, unlike the rash/negligent variants of Qatl-i-Khata.
els.legal: Qatl Bis-Sabab is the final, residual homicide category in this cluster, distinguished from Qatl-i-Khata by its causal structure rather than its mental-state structure: both categories share the complete absence of intent to cause death or harm, but Khata involves a mistake in the offender's own direct act (aiming wrong, or misidentifying the target), while Bis-Sabab involves an unlawful act that merely becomes a cause of death through some intervening chain of events, without the offender's own conduct being the direct, proximate physical cause in the way a gunshot or blow would be. The pit-digging Illustration is instructive precisely because the unlawful act (digging an unauthorised pit in a public way) and the resulting death are separated by an intervening event entirely outside the offender's control — the victim's own decision to walk that route and fall in.
els.cite: S.321 and S.322 PPC and S.321's own Illustration — official text. Cross-reference General Explanations S.43 (Illegal — includes anything prohibited by law or furnishing ground for civil action) — the 'unlawful act' triggering S.321 liability draws on this same broad definitional category.

sq.q: A unlawfully digs a pit in a public thoroughfare, with no intention whatsoever of harming anyone — perhaps purely for some unrelated unlawful purpose. B, passing by some time later, falls into the pit and dies. Apply S.321 and its own Illustration. What is A liable for?
sq.reveal: This is the Code's own Illustration to S.321 directly: A has committed Qatl Bis-Sabab. The unlawful act (digging the unauthorised pit) became a cause of B's death, even though A never intended harm to B or anyone else, and even though the death occurred through an intervening chain of events (B's later, independent decision to walk that route) rather than through any direct physical act by A against B.
sq.cite: S.321 PPC Illustration — unlawful pit-digging becoming a cause of an unrelated passer-by's later death.

sq.q: Why does S.322 provide for Diyat liability only, with no Ta'zir imprisonment component at all — contrasting with S.319's proviso allowing up to 5 years' Ta'zir for rash/negligent Khata, or S.320's mandatory imprisonment for negligent-driving Khata?
sq.reveal: The structural difference reflects the more attenuated causal and culpability profile of Bis-Sabab compared to even the rash/negligent variants of Khata. In Khata, the offender's own direct act causes death through a mistake in execution or belief — there is at least a direct causal link between the offender's conduct and the result, even without intent. In Bis-Sabab, the offender's unlawful act merely 'becomes a cause' of death through some further intervening sequence of events, a more remote causal relationship. The legislature appears to have calibrated S.322's pure-Diyat punishment to this more attenuated culpability, reserving the additional Ta'zir imprisonment options for the more directly-caused Khata categories.
sq.cite: S.322 PPC (Diyat only) vs S.319/320 PPC (Diyat plus Ta'zir options) — calibrated to the more attenuated causal chain in Bis-Sabab compared to Khata.

sq.q: Does the unlawful act underlying Qatl Bis-Sabab need to be one that is itself dangerous or likely to cause harm, or can any unlawful act — however minor or unrelated to physical safety — trigger S.321 liability if it happens to become a cause of death?
sq.reveal: S.321's text requires only that the act be unlawful and that it 'becomes a cause for the death of another person' — it does not on its face require the unlawful act to be inherently dangerous or safety-related. The pit-digging Illustration involves an act (unauthorised excavation in a public way) that does carry an inherent physical safety risk, but the section's wording is broader than that specific example might suggest, focusing on the causal outcome (the unlawful act becoming a cause of death) rather than requiring the unlawful act to fall into any particular category of inherently dangerous conduct.
sq.cite: S.321 PPC — textual focus on causal outcome rather than requiring the underlying unlawful act to be inherently dangerous.

els.name: Causing Death of Person Other Than the Person Whose Death Was Intended
els.sub: S.301 PPC — transferred intent for Qatl-i-Amd
els.desc: Where a person, by doing anything which he intends or knows to be likely to cause death, causes the death of any person whose death he neither intends nor knows himself to be likely to cause, that act is liable for Qatl-i-Amd — the unintended victim's death is treated identically to an intended killing.
els.legal: S.301 is the PPC's codified transferred-intent doctrine for homicide: the offender's culpable mental state attaches to whichever death actually results from the dangerous act, regardless of who the offender specifically had in mind. This operates as a statutory gloss directly on S.300's Niyyat requirement — the mental state required by S.300 need only be directed at causing death generally (or at the specific dangerous act), not at the specific person who ends up dying.
els.cite: S.301 PPC — official text. Cross-reference Murder S.300 Niyyat element — S.301 confirms the required mental state need not be aimed at the actual victim.

sq.q: A fires a gun intending to kill B. The bullet misses B and instead kills C, a bystander A never intended to harm and did not know was present. Apply S.301. Is A liable for C's death as Qatl-i-Amd?
sq.reveal: Yes. S.301 directly covers this fact pattern: A intended (and knew likely) to cause death through the act of firing the gun; the death that actually resulted (C's) was neither intended nor known likely by A, but S.301 deems the act 'liable for Qatl-i-Amd' regardless. A's S.300 mental state, formed with B as the target, transfers to C as the actual victim.
sq.cite: S.301 PPC — transferred intent for unintended victim's death.

sq.q: How does S.301 relate to S.300's Niyyat clauses already built in the Murder module — does S.301 require a separate, additional mental state analysis, or does it simply redirect the existing S.300 analysis to a different victim?
sq.reveal: S.301 does not create a separate mental state requirement — it redirects the existing S.300 analysis. The S.300 Niyyat clauses (intent to cause death, intent to cause likely-fatal injury, knowledge of imminently dangerous act, etc.) are assessed exactly as they would be for the intended victim; S.301 then simply confirms that whichever death actually results, even an entirely unintended one, is treated as Qatl-i-Amd on the strength of that same mental state. The Niyyat analysis itself does not change; only the identity of the victim it attaches to changes.
sq.cite: S.301 PPC operating on top of, not alongside, the S.300 Niyyat framework.

sq.q: A intends to poison B's food. By complete accident unrelated to A's act, C eats from a different dish and dies of an unrelated cause at the same time. Does S.301 make A liable for C's death?
sq.reveal: No. S.301 requires that the death actually be caused by the act A intended or knew likely to cause death — here, C's death was caused by an entirely unrelated event, not by A's poisoning act at all. S.301 transfers intent across victims where the dangerous act itself causes the unintended death; it does not create liability for coincidental, causally unconnected deaths that happen to occur near in time to the accused's act.
sq.cite: S.301 PPC — requires the unintended victim's death to actually be caused by the accused's dangerous act, not merely coincide with it.

els.name: Qatl Committed Under Ikrah-i-Tam or Ikrah-i-Naqis
els.sub: S.303 PPC — duress-induced killing, two grades of compulsion
els.desc: Where Qatl is committed under Ikrah-i-Tam (complete duress — defined in S.299(g) as fear of instant death, instant permanent organ impairment, or instant sodomy/zina-bil-jabr threatened against the person, their spouse, or a close blood relative), the direct killer is punished with 10–25 years' imprisonment, while the person who caused the Ikrah-i-Tam is punished as if they themselves had committed that kind of Qatl. Where committed under Ikrah-i-Naqis (lesser duress not amounting to Ikrah-i-Tam), the direct killer is punished for the kind of Qatl committed, while the person causing the lesser duress faces up to 10 years.
els.legal: S.303 is the PPC's codified duress defence specifically for homicide, operating through the two duress-grade definitions already established in S.299. The structural inversion is the key analytical point: under Ikrah-i-Tam, liability shifts substantially toward the person who coerced the killing rather than the person who physically performed it, while under the lesser Ikrah-i-Naqis, the direct killer retains primary liability for the kind of Qatl committed, with the coercer facing a separate, capped liability. This is conceptually adjacent to, but structurally distinct from, the General Exceptions S.94 compulsion defence — S.303 is Chapter XVI's own specialised duress provision for homicide specifically, rather than relying on the general compulsion exception, which itself does not apply to offences punishable with death.
els.cite: S.303 PPC and S.299(g)/(h) definitions of Ikrah-i-Tam and Ikrah-i-Naqis — official text. Cross-reference General Exceptions — S.94 compulsion defence does not apply to capital offences, making S.303 the operative provision specifically for duress-induced killing.

sq.q: A threatens to immediately kill B's spouse unless B kills Z. B, genuinely believing the threat to be immediate and credible, kills Z. Apply S.303. Who bears primary liability, and for what?
sq.reveal: This satisfies S.299(g)'s definition of Ikrah-i-Tam — fear of instant death threatened against B's spouse, a person within the protected category. Under S.303(a), B (the direct killer) is punished with imprisonment of 10–25 years, while A (who caused the Ikrah-i-Tam) is punished 'for the kind of qatl committed' — meaning A faces liability as if A had committed the Qatl directly, which could mean exposure to the full S.302 Qisas/Ta'zir framework depending on which S.300 Niyyat clause is satisfied on these facts.
sq.cite: S.303(a) PPC and S.299(g) Ikrah-i-Tam definition.

sq.q: Why does S.303 shift primary liability toward the coercer under Ikrah-i-Tam specifically, rather than simply treating duress as a complete defence for the direct killer in all cases?
sq.reveal: The structure reflects a deliberate moral and causal judgment: under the severe, complete duress of Ikrah-i-Tam (immediate threat to life, bodily integrity, or sexual violation against the person or close relatives), the direct killer's free will is treated as having been overborne to a degree that substantially shifts moral and legal responsibility to the coercer, though not entirely — the direct killer still faces a real punishment (10–25 years), reflecting that taking a life under duress remains a serious wrong even where culpability is reduced. This is a middle position between treating duress as a complete defence (full acquittal) and ignoring it entirely.
sq.cite: S.303 PPC — structural rationale for the liability-shifting mechanism under complete duress.

sq.q: How does S.303's Ikrah-i-Tam framework interact with General Exceptions S.94 (act done under compulsion)? Could a defendant facing a duress-induced murder charge rely on S.94 instead of S.303?
sq.reveal: S.94 explicitly excludes offences punishable with death from its scope, meaning it cannot operate as a complete defence to a Qatl charge regardless of how severe the duress was. S.303 is therefore the specific, operative duress provision for homicide — it does not offer a complete defence either, but instead recalibrates punishment and shifts substantial liability to the coercer. A defendant facing a duress-induced Qatl charge must rely on S.303's specific framework, not the general S.94 exception, which is simply unavailable for this category of offence.
sq.cite: S.303 PPC vs General Exceptions S.94 — S.94's express exclusion of capital offences makes S.303 the operative duress provision for Qatl.

els.name: Proof of Qatl-i-Amd Liable to Qisas
els.sub: S.304 PPC — the strict evidentiary gateway
els.desc: Proof of Qatl-i-Amd for Qisas purposes must be in one of two forms: a voluntary and true confession made before a competent court, or evidence as provided in Article 17 of the Qanun-e-Shahadat Order 1984. These same proof requirements apply, with necessary modification, to hurt liable to Qisas.
els.legal: This is the statutory anchor for the strict eyewitness/confession standard already discussed in the Murder module's S.302 Qisas branch — S.304 is the specific provision that S.302(b)'s phrase 'proof in either of the forms specified in section 304 is not available' refers back to. Article 17 of the Qanun-e-Shahadat Order is itself a QSO (Evidence law) provision rather than a PPC provision, meaning the precise content of the eyewitness-qualification requirements sits in a different statute entirely — this is a genuine cross-code dependency that the future QSO/CrPC Navigator app would need to address in full, since this app's PPC-only scope means Article 17's detailed content cannot be fully built here.
els.cite: S.304 PPC — official text. Direct cross-reference: Article 17, Qanun-e-Shahadat Order 1984 (P.O. No. 10 of 1984) — outside this app's PPC-only scope; flagged here for completeness rather than built in full.

sq.q: The Murder module's S.302 Qisas element states that Qisas requires proof 'in either of the forms specified in section 304.' What are those two forms specifically, now that S.304 has been verified directly?
sq.reveal: The two forms are: (1) a voluntary and true confession made by the accused before a court competent to try the offence, or (2) evidence as provided in Article 17 of the Qanun-e-Shahadat Order 1984 — which sets out the specific eyewitness-testimony requirements for Hudood-category offences, commonly understood to require multiple qualified witnesses for the strictest evidentiary categories. S.304 itself does not spell out Article 17's content; it simply incorporates that QSO provision by reference as the second permissible proof route alongside confession.
sq.cite: S.304(1)(a)-(b) PPC — confession or Article 17 QSO evidence as the only two routes to Qisas-level proof.

sq.q: Why does S.304(2) extend the same strict proof requirements to hurt liable to Qisas, rather than leaving hurt offences to standard criminal evidence rules?
sq.reveal: Since Qisas for hurt operates on the same retaliation-in-kind logic as Qisas for Qatl (causing similar injury to the offender as was caused to the victim, per S.299(k)), the legislature applied the identical strict evidentiary gateway to both categories — a punishment as severe and irreversible as Qisas, whether for death or for bodily injury, was judged to require the same heightened proof threshold regardless of which Chapter XVI offence is being punished through that mechanism.
sq.cite: S.304(2) PPC — extending Qisas proof standards from Qatl to hurt 'mutatis mutandis.'

sq.q: If the prosecution cannot meet either S.304 proof route for Qisas, does the accused go free, or is there a fallback punishment track?
sq.reveal: There is a fallback — this is precisely the function of S.302(b) Ta'zir, already built in the Murder module: where Qisas proof under S.304 is not available, the court may still convict and sentence under the Ta'zir track using standard criminal evidence (circumstantial evidence, forensics, credible single-witness testimony), with death or life imprisonment still available as Ta'zir, having regard to the facts and circumstances. S.304's strict proof requirement governs only the Qisas track specifically; it does not create a general evidentiary barrier to conviction for Qatl-i-Amd as a whole.
sq.cite: S.304 PPC governing the Qisas track only; S.302(b) Ta'zir as the standard-evidence fallback, already built in the Murder module.

els.name: Wali — Who Holds the Right of Qisas
els.sub: S.305 PPC — defining the party entitled to claim or waive Qisas
els.desc: In a case of Qatl, the wali is the heirs of the victim according to their personal law — but, since the 2005 amendment, this expressly excludes the accused or convict themselves where the Qatl-i-Amd was committed in the name or on the pretext of honour. Where there is no heir, the Government itself becomes the wali.
els.legal: S.305's 2005 amendment closes a specific, serious loophole: without it, an offender in an honour-killing case could in principle be, or control, the very wali entitled to waive or compound Qisas against themselves — for example a father killing a son, where the father might otherwise be among the victim's heirs entitled to forgive the killing. The amendment expressly removes the accused or convict from the pool of eligible wali specifically in honour-killing cases, ensuring the waiver/compounding mechanism in S.309/310 cannot be manipulated by the offender forgiving themselves through their own status as an heir.
els.cite: S.305 PPC, proviso added by Act I of 2005 — official text. Direct structural counterpart to S.311's fasad-fil-arz override and the Honour Killing Reform Arc already built in this app's Reform Arcs section.

sq.q: A father kills his own son in the name of honour. The father is technically one of the son's heirs under personal law. Apply S.305's 2005 proviso. Can the father, as wali, waive his own right of Qisas against himself?
sq.reveal: No. The 2005 proviso to S.305 expressly excludes the accused or convict from the definition of wali where the Qatl-i-Amd was committed in the name or on the pretext of honour — this directly closes the self-forgiveness loophole on these facts. The father cannot occupy the wali role with respect to a killing he himself committed under the honour-killing characterisation; some other eligible heir (or the Government, if none exists) becomes the wali instead.
sq.cite: S.305 PPC proviso (2005) — accused/convict excluded from wali status in honour-killing Qatl cases.

sq.q: How does S.305's exclusion of the accused-as-wali connect structurally to S.311's fasad-fil-arz override, already built in the Murder module? Are they doing the same work, or different work?
sq.reveal: They address the same underlying social problem — family-based manipulation of the Qisas/Diyat mechanism in honour killings — but through different mechanisms operating at different stages. S.305 operates at the threshold stage: it determines who is even eligible to be the wali in the first place, removing the offender from that pool entirely in honour cases. S.311 operates at a later stage, assuming a valid wali (someone other than the offender) has already waived or compounded Qisas, and then asks whether the state's independent Ta'zir power should override that waiver where fasad-fil-arz is attracted. S.305 prevents the offender from forgiving themselves; S.311 prevents even a genuine third-party wali's forgiveness from fully extinguishing punishment in honour cases.
sq.cite: S.305 vs S.311 PPC — threshold eligibility for wali status vs subsequent state override of a valid wali's waiver, both targeting honour-killing manipulation of the Qisas mechanism.

sq.q: If a victim of Qatl-i-Amd has no surviving heirs at all, who becomes the wali, and what practical effect does this have on the availability of waiver or compounding under S.309/310?
sq.reveal: S.305(b) provides that the Government becomes the wali where there is no heir. Practically, this affects S.309's waiver mechanism directly, since S.309(1)(a) expressly provides that the right of Qisas shall not be waived where the Government is the wali — meaning a victim with no surviving heirs effectively forecloses the waiver route entirely, leaving the Qisas/Ta'zir framework to operate without the possibility of private forgiveness extinguishing the right.
sq.cite: S.305(b) PPC (Government as wali where no heir exists) read with S.309(1)(a)'s express bar on waiver where the Government holds that status.

els.name: When Qatl-i-Amd Is Not Liable to Qisas, or Qisas Cannot Be Enforced
els.sub: S.306/307 PPC — categorical exclusions and enforcement bars
els.desc: S.306 lists three categories where Qatl-i-Amd is never liable to Qisas at all: where the offender is a minor or insane (with an anti-evasion proviso preventing a Qisas-liable offender from escaping liability by associating with a minor/insane co-offender); where the offender kills their own child or grandchild however remote; and where any wali of the victim is a direct descendant of the offender. S.307 separately lists three situations where Qisas, even though it would otherwise be available, cannot actually be enforced: the offender's death before execution; a wali's voluntary, duress-free waiver or compounding; or the right of Qisas devolving onto the offender themselves (or someone with no right of Qisas against the offender) through the death of the original wali.
els.legal: The distinction between S.306 (categorical non-liability) and S.307 (enforcement bars on an otherwise-valid right) matters analytically: S.306 means Qisas was never available on these facts in the first place, while S.307 means Qisas was validly established but subsequent events prevent its actual execution. The Code's own Illustrations to S.307(c) walk through the devolution scenario precisely — where the right of Qisas would devolve onto a person who is themselves the offender's own child (and therefore covered by the S.306(b)/(c) logic against descendants), the right simply cannot be enforced, since the person who would hold it cannot claim Qisas against their own parent.
els.cite: S.306 and S.307 PPC, including S.307's two worked Illustrations — official text.
els.ll: S.306 and S.307 PPC — official text

sq.q: A kills Z, who is the maternal uncle of A's own son B. Z has no wali except D, A's wife. D has the right of Qisas against A. D then dies, and the right of Qisas devolves onto her son B — who is also A's son. Apply S.307(c) and the Code's own Illustration. Can Qisas be enforced against A?
sq.reveal: No — this is the Code's own Illustration (i) to S.307 directly. Once the right of Qisas devolves onto B, B cannot claim Qisas against his own father A, since B himself is a direct descendant of the offender (the exact category S.306(c) excludes from holding Qisas against an ancestor). Because the right has devolved onto a person who has no right of Qisas against the offender, S.307(c) bars enforcement entirely, even though Qisas was validly established in the first place against A.
sq.cite: S.307(c) PPC and Illustration (i) — devolution onto a person with no right of Qisas against the offender.

sq.q: What is the practical difference between a case falling under S.306 (Qatl-i-Amd never liable to Qisas) and a case falling under S.307 (Qisas validly arises but cannot be enforced)? Does this distinction affect what punishment track ultimately applies?
sq.reveal: Both ultimately route the offender away from Qisas, but the underlying legal characterisation differs, which matters for downstream provisions: S.308 (punishment in Qatl-i-Amd not liable to Qisas) expressly addresses both situations — 'not liable to qisas under section 306 or the qisas is not enforceable under clause (c) of section 307' — directing the offender to Diyat liability (and potentially additional Ta'zir imprisonment) in either case. So while the analytical pathway differs (never-available vs validly-arising-but-barred), S.308 deliberately treats both outcomes identically for punishment purposes, channelling the offender to the same Diyat/Ta'zir framework regardless of which route led there.
sq.cite: S.306 vs S.307(c) PPC, both feeding into the identical S.308 punishment framework despite their different legal characterisations.

sq.q: A Qisas-liable adult offender deliberately recruits their own minor child to jointly participate in a killing, hoping the minor's involvement will somehow shield the adult from Qisas. Apply S.306(a)'s proviso. Does this strategy work?
sq.reveal: No. S.306(a)'s proviso explicitly anticipates and forecloses exactly this evasion strategy: 'where a person liable to qisas associates himself in the commission of the offence with a person not liable to qisas with the intention of saving himself from qisas, he shall not be exempted from qisas.' The adult offender's Qisas liability is expressly preserved despite the minor's involvement; the minor's own non-liability (due to age) does not transfer any protection to the adult co-offender who deliberately engineered that association.
sq.cite: S.306(a) PPC proviso — anti-evasion provision preventing a Qisas-liable offender from sheltering behind a non-liable co-offender.

els.name: Punishment in Qatl-i-Amd Not Liable to Qisas
els.sub: S.308 PPC — the Diyat/Ta'zir fallback framework
els.desc: Where an offender guilty of Qatl-i-Amd is not liable to Qisas under S.306, or Qisas is unenforceable under S.307(c), the offender is liable to Diyat — payable from the minor/insane offender's own property or by a court-determined person where applicable. Two further provisos extend exposure: where a minor had attained sufficient maturity, or an insane offender had a lucid interval, sufficient to realise the consequences of their act, they may additionally face up to 25 years' imprisonment as Ta'zir; and where there is no wali other than the offender themselves, the offender faces up to 25 years' imprisonment as Ta'zir instead of Diyat. The court may also, regardless of these provisos, add further Ta'zir imprisonment up to 25 years on top of the Diyat liability, having regard to the facts and circumstances.
els.legal: S.308 is the structural bridge from S.306/307's categorical exclusions to an actual punishment outcome — without it, the categories in S.306/307 would simply describe when Qisas is unavailable without specifying what happens instead. The minor/insane provisos reflect a graduated approach to diminished capacity: full incapacity (a true minor or genuinely insane offender with no lucid interval) results in Diyat liability only, payable from their property or by a court-determined responsible party, with no Ta'zir imprisonment; but where sufficient maturity or a lucid interval existed at the time of the act, additional Ta'zir becomes available, recognising that capacity is not always absolute even within the minor/insane categories.
els.cite: S.308 PPC — official text. Cross-reference General Exceptions S.82/83 (child under 10 / child 10–14 of immature understanding) and S.84 (unsound mind) — S.308's maturity/lucid-interval provisos engage similar capacity-assessment logic to those General Exceptions, but operate specifically within the Diyat/Ta'zir punishment framework rather than as a complete defence.

sq.q: A 13-year-old commits Qatl-i-Amd. Medical and circumstantial evidence establishes the child had attained sufficient maturity to fully realise the consequences of the act. Apply S.308. What is the child's total exposure?
sq.reveal: The child is not liable to Qisas (S.306(a) excludes minors categorically), but under S.308's first proviso, since sufficient maturity to realise the consequences is established, the child faces Diyat liability (payable from their own property, per the main provision) AND may additionally be punished with imprisonment of either description up to 25 years as Ta'zir. The combination of Diyat plus potential 25-year Ta'zir for a minor who demonstrably understood their actions reflects the legislature's judgment that chronological minority alone does not automatically mean diminished capacity in every case.
sq.cite: S.308 PPC, first proviso — sufficient maturity in a minor offender opening additional Ta'zir exposure beyond Diyat.

sq.q: An offender guilty of Qatl-i-Amd has no wali other than themselves (for example, the only possible heir is the offender's own spouse, who happens also to be debarred or otherwise unavailable, leaving no other eligible wali). Apply S.308's third proviso. What punishment track applies instead of Diyat?
sq.reveal: Where the qisas is unenforceable under S.307(c) and there is no wali other than the offender, S.308's third proviso specifies the offender is liable to Diyat ONLY if there is a wali other than the offender — if there is no such other wali, the offender instead faces imprisonment of either description for a term up to 25 years as Ta'zir, in place of Diyat (since there is, on these facts, no one other than the offender to whom Diyat could properly be paid as compensation).
sq.cite: S.308 PPC, third proviso — Ta'zir substituting for Diyat where no wali other than the offender exists.

sq.q: Does S.308's main provision (Diyat liability) and its further sub-section (2) operate as alternatives, or can a court impose both Diyat and additional Ta'zir imprisonment on the same offender for the same Qatl-i-Amd?
sq.reveal: Both can be imposed cumulatively. S.308(2) explicitly states 'Notwithstanding anything contained in sub section (1), the court... may punish the offender with imprisonment... in addition to the punishment of diyat' — the 'notwithstanding' and 'in addition to' language confirms this is an additive power, not an alternative one. A court can impose the Diyat liability required by S.308(1) and separately add Ta'zir imprisonment up to 25 years under S.308(2), having regard to the facts and circumstances of the case, without this being treated as double punishment for the same wrong.
sq.cite: S.308(2) PPC — 'notwithstanding' and 'in addition to' confirming Diyat and Ta'zir imprisonment are cumulative, not alternative.

els.name: Waiver and Compounding of Qisas
els.sub: S.309/310 PPC — the heirs' two mechanisms for extinguishing Qisas
els.desc: S.309 allows an adult, sane wali to waive (Afw) their right of Qisas at any time, without any compensation — except where the Government is the wali, where the right vests in a minor or insane person, or (since 2016) where fasad-fil-arz is attracted, in which case S.311 governs instead. Multiple-wali and multiple-victim/offender scenarios are addressed: one wali's waiver does not affect another's right or entitlement to their diyat share. S.310 separately allows compounding (Sulh) — accepting Badal-i-Sulh (mutually agreed compensation) in exchange for relinquishing Qisas — with the 2005 proviso expressly barring a female from being given in marriage as Badal-i-Sulh, and the 2016 proviso again subjecting honour-killing-attracted cases to S.311 instead.
els.legal: These two sections, both substantially amended in 2005 and 2016, represent successive legislative tightening of the family-settlement mechanism in homicide cases. Waiver (S.309) is unconditional forgiveness with no compensation; compounding (S.310) is forgiveness in exchange for agreed compensation (Badal-i-Sulh), which need not be cash — the Explanation confirms it can be movable or immovable property or any Shariah-compliant form of value, though it cannot be a female given in marriage (the 2005 anti-vani/swara proviso, reinforced by the standalone S.310A offence). Both mechanisms are now explicitly subordinated to S.311 wherever fasad-fil-arz is attracted, meaning the 2016 amendments did not abolish family waiver/compounding but layered a state-override mechanism on top of it specifically for the gravest, honour-motivated cases.
els.cite: S.309 and S.310 PPC, as amended by Act I of 2005 and Act XLIII of 2016 — official text. Direct cross-reference: S.305 (excluding the offender from wali status in honour cases) and S.311 (state Ta'zir override), already built/corrected in the Murder module, plus the Honour Killing Reform Arc.
els.ll: S.309 and S.310 PPC — official text

sq.q: A victim of Qatl-i-Amd has two wali, X and Y. X waives the right of Qisas without compensation under S.309. Y does not waive. Apply S.309(2). What happens to Y's position?
sq.reveal: X's waiver does not affect Y's independent right of Qisas — S.309(2) expressly provides that any one wali may waive their own right, but the wali who does NOT waive remains entitled to their share of diyat. The two wali's positions are treated separately: X's unconditional forgiveness operates only as to X's own share/right, leaving Y's right of Qisas (or entitlement to diyat) fully intact.
sq.cite: S.309(2) PPC — one wali's waiver does not extinguish another wali's independent right or diyat entitlement.

sq.q: A wali agrees to compound their right of Qisas under S.310 in exchange for the offender's family giving a daughter in marriage to the wali's family, as Badal-i-Sulh. Apply S.310's 2005 proviso. Is this a valid compounding?
sq.reveal: No. S.310's express proviso, added in 2005, states 'a female shall not be given in marriage or otherwise in badal i sulh' — this directly prohibits using a female as the compensation in a Qisas-compounding arrangement. This proviso works together with the standalone offence under S.310A, which separately criminalises compelling a female into marriage as badla-e-sulh, wanni, or swara, carrying its own dedicated punishment (3–7 years plus substantial mandatory fine) independent of whether the underlying compounding attempt itself is treated as valid.
sq.cite: S.310 PPC 2005 proviso (female-as-compensation prohibition) and S.310A PPC (standalone offence for compelling such marriage).

sq.q: How do the 2016 provisos to S.309 and S.310 (both subjecting waiver/compounding to S.311 where fasad-fil-arz is attracted) actually operate in sequence with S.311 itself, already built in the Murder module? Walk through the logical order of analysis.
sq.reveal: The logical sequence is: first, S.305 determines who is even eligible to be wali (excluding the offender in honour cases); second, that valid wali may attempt to waive under S.309 or compound under S.310; third, both sections' 2016 provisos immediately flag that if fasad-fil-arz is attracted, the waiver/compounding does not operate to fully extinguish the matter — instead S.311 takes over, allowing the court to award Ta'zir (death, life, or up to 14 years generally, but mandatorily life imprisonment specifically where the honour proviso applies) regardless of the wali's waiver or compounding. The family-level forgiveness mechanism and the state-level Ta'zir override operate as sequential, not competing, layers — the wali's waiver/compounding is given effect for Qisas purposes, but S.311 ensures it cannot be the final word where honour-motivated killing is established.
sq.cite: S.309/S.310 2016 provisos operating sequentially with S.311 PPC (corrected this session in the Murder module) — family forgiveness layer followed by state Ta'zir override layer.

els.name: Fasad-fil-Arz — State Override of Waiver and Compounding
els.sub: S.311 PPC — the honour-killing/corruption-on-earth Ta'zir override
els.desc: Where all the wali do not waive or compound the right of Qisas, or where the principle of fasad-fil-arz is attracted, the court may punish an offender against whom the right of Qisas has been waived or compounded with death, imprisonment for life, or imprisonment of either description up to fourteen years as Ta'zir — provided that if the offence was committed in the name or on the pretext of honour, the punishment shall be imprisonment for life specifically, not the general 14-year ceiling.
els.legal: S.311 sits precisely between S.309/310's waiver-and-compounding mechanism (just built) and S.312's anti-vigilante provision (next) for a structural reason: it is the state's override sitting on top of the private settlement those two sections create. Two distinct triggers operate disjunctively — incomplete waiver/compounding by all wali, OR fasad-fil-arz being attracted — either alone is sufficient to engage this section. The honour-killing proviso then collapses the court's general Ta'zir discretion (death/life/up to 14 years) to a single mandated outcome: life imprisonment, no more, no less. This directly closes the loophole S.305's 2005 proviso (already built, two cards back) opened the door to recognising: where family members hold the wali's forgiveness power, S.311 ensures that forgiveness cannot be the final word in honour-motivated killing. See the Down the Rabbit Hole module, Essay 3, for the deeper jurisprudential reasoning — why fasad-fil-arz exists specifically to stop wealth or family power converting Diyat into a purchasable exit.
els.cite: S.311 PPC — fasad-fil-arz provision added by Criminal Law Amendment Act 2016. Cross-reference S.305 PPC (this module, wali exclusion) and S.309/310 PPC (this module, waiver/compounding) — S.311 is the state override sitting on top of both.

sq.q: A husband kills his wife claiming honour. The wife's family forgives him and accepts Diyat under S.309/310. After 2016, is he free?
sq.reveal: Not necessarily. The court must independently examine whether this constitutes fasad-fil-arz under S.311. If the killing was in the name of honour, the court has an independent obligation to consider Ta'zir punishment regardless of the family's forgiveness — and where the honour proviso applies, that punishment is specifically mandated as life imprisonment, not left to discretion.
sq.cite: S.311 PPC — 2016 amendment overriding family forgiveness in honour-motivated Qatl.

sq.q: How does S.311's honour proviso differ from its general Ta'zir tier in terms of the court's sentencing discretion?
sq.reveal: The general tier (no honour-motivation finding) leaves the court genuine discretion across death, life imprisonment, or up to 14 years. The honour proviso removes that discretion entirely — once the offence is found to have been committed in the name or on the pretext of honour, life imprisonment is the only available outcome, neither more severe (death) nor less (a term of years).
sq.cite: S.311 PPC — general discretionary tier vs the honour proviso's single mandated outcome.

sq.q: Why does S.311 sit structurally between S.309/310 (waiver/compounding) and S.312 (subsequent killing of a forgiven convict) rather than elsewhere in this module?
sq.reveal: S.311 only makes sense once a waiver or compounding has actually occurred — it is the state's check on the private settlement S.309/310 just created. S.312, immediately following, addresses what happens if a wali takes matters into their own hands after that same settlement. The sequence S.309/310 → S.311 → S.312 traces one continuous narrative: private forgiveness, the state's override of it, and the consequence of a wali acting outside the process entirely.
sq.cite: S.311 PPC's structural position — the state override sitting directly between the private settlement mechanism and the anti-vigilante provision that follows it.

els.name: Subsequent Killing of a Forgiven Convict; Right and Execution of Qisas
els.sub: S.312/313/314 PPC — procedural mechanics after waiver, and the execution process itself
els.desc: S.312 addresses a specific scenario: where a wali kills a convict against whom Qisas had already been waived (S.309) or compounded (S.310), that wali themselves faces Qisas if they had personally waived/compounded or knew of another's waiver/compounding, or Diyat if they had no such knowledge. S.313 distributes the right of Qisas itself: a sole wali holds it alone; multiple wali each hold it independently; where there is no wali, the Government holds it; and where the only wali is a minor or insane, their father (or paternal grandfather) holds the right on their behalf, with Government stepping in only if no such guardian exists or has been court-appointed. S.314 governs the mechanics of execution itself: carried out by a government functionary causing the convict's death as the court directs, requiring all wali (or their authorised representatives) to be present, with a specific two-year postponement provision for a pregnant convict, during which she may be released on bail or treated as serving simple imprisonment.
els.legal: S.312 is a deliberate anti-vigilante provision operating after the fact: it prevents a wali from circumventing their own (or another wali's) waiver or compounding by simply killing the forgiven convict outside the formal Qisas process — doing so exposes that wali to Qisas themselves if they had knowledge of the waiver, treating their unauthorised killing as itself a fresh act of Qatl rather than a legitimate continuation of the original right. S.313's distribution rules and S.314's procedural safeguards (mandatory presence of all wali, the pregnancy postponement) collectively ensure Qisas execution remains a tightly controlled, judicially supervised state function rather than something any individual wali can carry out unilaterally or without proper process.
els.cite: S.312, S.313, and S.314 PPC — official text.
els.ll: S.312, S.313, S.314 PPC — official text

sq.q: A wali waives the right of Qisas against a convict under S.309, accepting that the convict will instead face Diyat liability. Some time later, that same wali, frustrated by the process, personally kills the convict outside any court-sanctioned execution. Apply S.312. What is the wali's liability?
sq.reveal: Under S.312(a), since the wali had personally waived the right of Qisas (meaning they had direct knowledge of the waiver, having made it themselves), the wali is liable to Qisas for this subsequent, unauthorised killing of the convict. S.312 treats this killing as a fresh wrong in its own right — the original waiver does not somehow authorise or excuse the wali taking matters into their own hands later; instead it specifically exposes the wali themselves to the most severe punishment available for their vigilante act.
sq.cite: S.312(a) PPC — wali's personal liability to Qisas for unauthorised killing of a convict they themselves had waived against.

sq.q: A victim's only wali is their seven-year-old child, with no father or paternal grandfather alive, and no guardian has been appointed by any court. Apply S.313(2). Who holds the right of Qisas on the child's behalf?
sq.reveal: The Government holds the right of Qisas on the minor wali's behalf. S.313(2)(b) provides that ordinarily the father or, if not alive, the paternal grandfather of a minor or insane wali holds the right on their behalf — but the proviso to that sub-section specifically addresses the scenario where the minor/insane wali has no father or paternal grandfather alive AND no court-appointed guardian, in which case the Government steps into that representative role.
sq.cite: S.313(2)(b) PPC and its proviso — Government as the representative holder of Qisas where no family guardian exists for a minor/insane wali.

sq.q: A convict sentenced to Qisas is discovered to be pregnant shortly before the scheduled execution. Apply S.314(3). What happens to the timing of the execution, and what is her status during the intervening period?
sq.reveal: The court, in consultation with an authorised medical officer, may postpone execution of Qisas for up to two years after the child's birth. During that postponement period, she may be released on bail upon furnishing security to the court's satisfaction, or, if not so released, she is dealt with as if sentenced to simple imprisonment — meaning the more severe consequence of Qisas itself is held in abeyance, and her treatment during the interim defaults to the comparatively lighter simple-imprisonment regime rather than continued exposure to the death sentence during pregnancy and the child's earliest infancy.
sq.cite: S.314(3) PPC — pregnancy-based postponement of Qisas execution, with bail or simple-imprisonment treatment during the postponement period.

els.name: Value of Diyat
els.sub: S.323 PPC — the statutory valuation formula
els.desc: The court fixes the value of Diyat, subject to the Injunctions of Islam as laid down in the Holy Quran and Sunnah, having regard to the financial position of the convict and the heirs of the victim — but the value can never be less than the value of 30,630 grams of silver. The Federal Government, by Gazette notification, declares the current value of silver annually (or on such other date as it deems fit), which then governs the financial year.
els.legal: S.323 is the mechanism that converts the Diyat liability imposed throughout the preceding sections (S.308, 316, 319, 320, 322, and others) into an actual monetary figure. The structure combines a religious-law floor (the silver-weight minimum, rooted in classical Islamic jurisprudence on blood money) with judicial discretion bounded by the parties' financial circumstances, and an administrative mechanism (annual Gazette notification of silver's market value) that allows the fixed silver-weight figure to track inflation and currency fluctuation automatically without requiring fresh legislation each time the underlying value needs updating.

sq.q: Why does S.323 fix the Diyat floor as a quantity of silver (30,630 grams) rather than a fixed rupee amount, given that almost every other fine and compensation figure elsewhere in the PPC (such as S.67's colonial-era rupee figures) is stated in fixed currency terms?
sq.reveal: Anchoring the floor to a quantity of silver, combined with the Federal Government's annual Gazette notification of silver's current value, allows the Diyat minimum to automatically track currency depreciation and inflation over time without requiring legislative amendment — directly solving the exact problem that has left S.67's 1860 rupee figures essentially symbolic and unrevised for over 165 years, as already discussed in the Punishments module. S.323's mechanism is a structural solution to the same problem S.67 illustrates as a cautionary example.
sq.cite: S.323 PPC silver-weight mechanism, contrasted with S.67 PPC's unrevised colonial rupee figures (Punishments module, Statutory Interpretation cross-reference).

sq.q: Can a court set Diyat below the silver-weight floor if the convict's financial position is genuinely too poor to pay even that minimum amount?
sq.reveal: S.323's text states the value 'shall not be less than the value of thirty thousand six hundred and thirty grams of silver' as an absolute floor — the convict's financial position is a factor the court considers in fixing the value (presumably relevant to how much above that floor, and on what payment schedule, the Diyat is set, connecting to S.331's instalment-payment mechanism), but the section's plain language does not create an exception allowing the court to set Diyat below the floor itself on affordability grounds. Inability to pay engages the separate mechanisms in S.331 (instalments, jail pending payment, bail on security, or recovery from the estate after death) rather than a reduction of the underlying Diyat value itself.
sq.cite: S.323 PPC — absolute silver-weight floor, with affordability addressed through S.331's payment mechanisms rather than a reduction of the Diyat value itself.

sq.q: S.323 directs the court to fix Diyat 'subject to the Injunctions of Islam as laid down in the Holy Quran and Sunnah.' Does this mean every individual Diyat calculation must independently re-derive its value from religious texts, or does the silver-weight floor itself already represent that religious-law determination?
sq.reveal: The silver-weight floor (30,630 grams) is itself the legislature's codification of the classical Islamic Diyat valuation, meaning courts do not need to independently re-derive the figure from primary religious sources in each case — the statutory floor already embodies that determination. The court's task under S.323 is to fix the actual value (which may exceed the floor) having regard to the convict's and heirs' financial position, operating within the boundary the legislature has already set by codifying the religious-law minimum into a specific, administratively-updatable figure.
sq.cite: S.323 PPC — the silver-weight floor as the legislature's own codification of the religious-law Diyat valuation, not a starting point for independent re-derivation in each case.

els.name: Disbursement and Payment of Diyat
els.sub: S.330/331 PPC — the procedural mechanics of actually paying Diyat
els.desc: S.330 directs that Diyat be disbursed among the victim's heirs according to their respective shares in inheritance, with a proviso that a heir who forgoes their share simply has Diyat not recovered to that extent (rather than redistributing their share to other heirs). S.331 governs the actual payment mechanics: Diyat may be paid in a lump sum or in instalments spread over up to 5 years from the date of final judgment; a convict who fails to pay within that period may be jailed and treated as serving simple imprisonment until paid in full, or released on bail/security/parole as prescribed; and where the convict dies before paying, the outstanding Diyat is recovered from their estate.
els.legal: These two sections complete the Diyat framework by addressing what happens after the value has been fixed under S.323: how it is divided among multiple heirs (S.330), and how actual payment is enforced over time (S.331). The estate-recovery provision in S.331(3) mirrors the structurally similar rule already built in the Punishments module's S.70 (fine surviving the offender's death as a charge against their estate) — both reflect the same underlying principle that certain financial obligations imposed by criminal sentencing are treated analogously to ordinary civil debts that survive the obligor's death, rather than being extinguished by it.
els.cite: S.330 and S.331 PPC — official text. Cross-reference Punishments module S.70 PPC (fine surviving the offender's death, recoverable from estate) — structurally parallel principle applied to Diyat.

sq.q: A victim has three heirs entitled to equal one-third shares of Diyat. One heir voluntarily forgoes their share entirely. Apply S.330's proviso. Do the other two heirs split that forgone share between them, or does it simply go unrecovered?
sq.reveal: It simply goes unrecovered — S.330's proviso states that where a heir forgoes their share, 'the diyat shall not be recovered to the extent of his share,' meaning the total Diyat amount actually collected is reduced by that heir's portion, rather than the forgone share being redistributed and recovered on behalf of the remaining heirs. The other two heirs continue to receive their own original one-third shares each; they do not receive any uplift from the forgone third.
sq.cite: S.330 PPC proviso — a forgone heir's share reduces the total amount recovered, rather than being redistributed to the remaining heirs.

sq.q: A convict is ordered to pay Diyat in instalments over 5 years under S.331(1). The convict dies in year 3, having paid only half the total amount. Apply S.331(3). What happens to the remaining unpaid Diyat?
sq.reveal: The remaining unpaid portion is recovered from the convict's estate, per S.331(3): 'Where a convict dies before the payment of diyat or any part thereof, it shall be recovered from his estate.' The convict's death partway through the instalment schedule does not extinguish the remaining obligation — it converts into a claim against whatever estate the convict leaves behind, conceptually parallel to how an unpaid fine under the Punishments module's S.70 survives the offender's death as a charge against their estate.
sq.cite: S.331(3) PPC — estate recovery of unpaid Diyat upon the convict's death, structurally parallel to S.70 PPC's fine-survives-death rule.

sq.q: A convict simply refuses to pay Diyat despite having the financial means to do so, and the instalment period under S.331(1) has now expired. Apply S.331(2). What are the court's options?
sq.reveal: Under S.331(2), the convict may be kept in jail and dealt with as if sentenced to simple imprisonment until the Diyat is paid in full, OR released on bail if they furnish security (or surety) equivalent to the amount of Diyat to the court's satisfaction, OR released on parole as may be prescribed by rule. The section gives the court a menu of enforcement mechanisms rather than a single mandatory consequence, allowing some flexibility in how non-payment is addressed depending on the convict's circumstances and the security they can offer.
sq.cite: S.331(2) PPC — jail-as-simple-imprisonment, bail-on-security, or parole as the three available enforcement responses to non-payment after the instalment period expires.
